title: WATCH: How the Seven-Pronged Pro-Life Political Strategy Has Failed
author: James Silberman
orientation: A short post promoting a Bradley Pierce talk from the Abolition Now Conference that catalogs the seven planks of mainstream pro-life political strategy and uses 47 years of Supreme Court history and abortion-rate data to argue the strategy has produced no measurable progress. The article frames incrementalism as not merely slow but failing on its own stated terms.
audiences: Pro-life voter weighing the Republican-judicial strategy
Abolitionist activist building the case against incrementalism
Christian pastor or layperson rethinking political strategy
Pro-life policy staffer defending incremental regulation

questions.question: What specific case did Bradley Pierce make at the Abolition Now Conference?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist building the case against incrementalism
questions.answer: Pierce systematically walked through the seven planks of the mainstream pro-life political strategy and showed that, after nearly five decades, this combined approach has produced essentially nothing in the way of substantive progress against abortion.
questions.quote: Bradley Pierce, a constitutional attorney and director of Abolish Abortion Texas, presented on the seven aspects of the incremental pro-life strategy and how this seven-pronged strategy has made little to no progress in 47 years.

questions.question: Hasn't the GOP-Supreme-Court pipeline been blocked by Democratic appointees?
questions.audience: Pro-life voter weighing the Republican-judicial strategy
questions.answer: No. Republicans have controlled the Supreme Court for almost the entire Roe era, and the very justices who issued and reaffirmed Roe were replaced by Republican-appointed justices, undercutting the premise that more Republican presidents will fix the court.
questions.quote: except for the few months between Scalia’s death and Gorsuch’s appointment, Republicans have held a majority of the supreme court for the entire 47 years since Roe was decided

questions.question: What does Planned Parenthood v. Casey reveal about the Republican-judges strategy?
questions.answer: Casey shows the strategy can deliver a court packed with Republican appointees and still produce a full reaffirmation of legal abortion, demolishing the assumption that party affiliation predicts how a justice will rule on Roe.
questions.quote: in 1992, a supreme court made up of eight Republican appointees and a pro-life democrat upheld legal abortion in full in the *Planned Parenthood v. Casey* opinion

questions.question: Among the current justices, who actually opposes Roe on the merits?
questions.answer: Only Clarence Thomas has clearly stated fundamental opposition to Roe in writing, and the other Republican appointees, including Alito and Roberts, have declined to join him, suggesting the supposed pro-life majority on the court is illusory.
questions.quote: Of the five justices on the current supreme court, only Clarence Thomas has ever indicated that he was fundamentally opposed to the *Roe* decision.

questions.question: Why is Thomas reportedly voting against hearing abortion cases?
questions.answer: Pierce reads Thomas's reluctance as a signal that Thomas himself believes at least one of his Republican-appointed colleagues would side with Roe if a case were granted, making any new ruling more likely to entrench than dismantle the precedent.
questions.quote: Thomas knows that one or more of his Republican-appointed colleagues supports *Roe v. Wade*.

questions.question: Aren't incremental laws at least chipping away at Roe in the courts?
questions.audience: Pro-life policy staffer defending incremental regulation
questions.answer: No. Of the 37 pro-life laws that have actually reached the Supreme Court since 1973, every single one has been turned back, with Roe upheld in full each time and under Republican-majority courts at that.
questions.quote: Hundreds of pro-life laws have been passed since 1973, and 37 of them have gone before the supreme court. Yet not one iota of the *Roe* decision has been rolled back.

questions.question: Even if Roe stands, aren't incremental laws still saving babies by lowering the abortion rate?
questions.audience: Pro-life policy staffer defending incremental regulation
questions.answer: The data does not support that claim. Heavily regulated pro-life states are not seeing significantly steeper declines than pro-abortion states, and in some comparisons states with the most permissive abortion laws are dropping faster than the most regulated ones.
questions.quote: the abortion rate is actually dropping faster in New York than it is in Texas. That’s despite Texas having numerous incremental pro-life regulations and New York having the most pro-abortion laws in the country.

questions.question: What does the author say Christians should do in response?
questions.audience: Christian pastor or layperson rethinking political strategy
questions.answer: He calls for repudiating the incrementalist posture as unbiblical and timid, and instead openly defying Roe by demanding the immediate, total abolition of abortion rather than waiting for the court's permission.
questions.quote: We must put an end to the unBiblical, obsequious, timid pro-life strategy of incrementalism. We’ve got to stand up to the supreme court and the pro-life establishment to demand abortion’s immediate abolition in defiance of the horrifically evil and openly unconstitutionally *Roe v. Wade* opinion.

questions.question: Why does the article urge readers to share the underlying video so widely?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist building the case against incrementalism
questions.answer: Because persuading people to consider an alternative strategy first requires convincing them the dominant one is genuinely failing, and Pierce's empirical walk-through of court appointments and case outcomes is meant to do exactly that.
questions.quote: To understand why a different strategy is needed, it’s helpful for people to know that the currently strategy isn’t working.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Companion piece by the same author that lays out the principled objections to incremental bills, complementing this article's empirical 'it doesn't work' argument.

related.title: Supreme Court Idolatry at Texas Alliance for Life
related.reason: Develops the same critique of pro-life faith in the Supreme Court that Pierce's seven-pronged strategy depends on.

related.title: Supreme Court Once Again Rejects Pro-Life Regulation
related.reason: A real-world data point added shortly after this article showing yet another pro-life regulation struck down by the court, reinforcing the 0-for-37 pattern cited here.

related.title: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
related.reason: Argues that the very kind of court loss this article warns about should finally end the strategy of waiting on the judiciary.

related.title: Four Problems With David Barton's Incrementalism Apologia
related.reason: Engages a leading public defense of incrementalism, useful for readers persuaded by this article who want to evaluate counter-arguments.

related.title: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
related.reason: Distills the strategic dichotomy this article assumes, giving newer readers a framework for the immediatist alternative Pierce is defending.
